manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: wall, time, ivory, horn, skyscraper, dwarf, spider, pound, plastic.
Your opponent's team words are: scientist, foot, fan, hawk, box, conductor, card, himalayas.
Distractor words are: america, superhero, centaur, forest, life, ham, dragon.
The kill words are: shoe.

We need to consider the partner's perspective: they will be given a clue and then need to guess words from the list (the whole set?) Usually in Codenames, the partner sees the list of all words (both teams, distractors, kill) and must guess the words that belong to their team. They guess one word at a time, and the clue giver can indicate how many words are related (the number). In this variant, we are to give a clue and a list of targets. The partner will try to guess those target words. If they guess a word not in the target list, but belonging to opponent or distractor, turn ends. If they guess the kill word, game ends.

Thus we need to give a clue that is associated with the target words, but not with other words.

We need to consider opponent words and distractor words. Let's list them again:

Opponent: scientist, foot, fan, hawk, box, conductor, card, himalayas.

Distractor: america, superhero, centaur, forest, life, ham, dragon.

Kill: shoe.

We need to avoid clues that might cause partner to guess any of these.

We also need to avoid the kill word "shoe". So we must not give "shoe" as clue, obviously.

We also must not give a clue that is in any list: that includes all words above.

Thus we cannot use "scientist", "foot", "fan", "hawk", "box", "conductor", "card", "himalayas", "america", "superhero", "centaur", "forest", "life", "ham", "dragon", "shoe". Also cannot use any of our own words: "wall", "time", "ivory", "horn", "skyscraper", "dwarf", "spider", "pound", "plastic".

Now we need to decide which target words we want to relate. Ideally we want to cover many of our words. However, the clue must be semantically related to the target words. The more words we try to relate, the more tenuous the association may become, making it harder for partner to guess. But we can also give multiple clues across multiple rounds. This is just one clue. But we need to give a clue now. The clue should be chosen to maximize the chance of partner guessing correctly without hitting opponent or distractor words.

We need to consider the partner's perspective: they will be given a clue and then need to guess words from the list (the whole set?) Usually in Codenames, the partner sees the list of all words (both teams, distractors, kill) and must guess the words that belong to their team. They guess one word at a time, and the clue giver can indicate how many words are related (the number). In this variant, we are to give a clue and a list of targets. The partner will try to guess those target words. If they guess a word not in the target list, but belonging to opponent or distractor, turn ends. If they guess the kill word, game ends.

Thus we need to give a clue that is associated with the target words, but not with other words.

We can also give a clue that has multiple associations, but we need to be careful.

Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:
